Nominal interest rate is the stated loan rate without inflation adjustment.
Scarcity forces individuals and societies to make difficult economic choices.
PPC model demonstrates resource allocation tradeoffs.
Absolute advantage: producing more output with same resources than others.
Price and quantity demanded move inversely; demand curves slope downward.
Higher prices incentivize producers to supply greater quantities of goods.
Market equilibrium occurs when quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.
GDP measures the economy's total final goods and services output.
GDP measures economic performance but excludes nonmarket transactions and has other limitations.
Unemployment rate measures percentage of labor force currently jobless.
CPI measures income change needed to maintain consistent living standards.
Unexpected inflation transfers wealth between groups like lenders and borrowers.
Business cycles result from aggregate supply and demand fluctuations affecting output and employment.
AD curve shows price level versus total quantity of goods and services demanded.
Autonomous expenditure changes trigger cascading effects on total expenditures and output.
SRAS curve shows price level and output quantity relationship in economy.
Long-run price and wage flexibility eliminates inflation-unemployment trade-off.
Short-run equilibrium occurs where AD and SRAS curves intersect.
Aggregate demand shocks shift output, employment, and prices directionally.
Long-run wage and price flexibility restores full employment after demand shocks.
Fiscal policy targets macroeconomic goals including full employment.
Automatic stabilizers moderate business cycles in both downturns and expansions.
Cash and demand deposits represent the most liquid money forms.
Money is any asset accepted as payment for goods and services.
Banks organize assets and liabilities using balance sheets.
Money demand inversely relates to nominal interest rate.
Central banks use monetary policy to achieve goals like price stability.
Loanable funds market shows interactions between savers and borrowers.
Sustained rapid money supply changes cause inflation or deflation.
Budget surplus/deficit equals tax revenues minus spending and transfers.
Deficit governments borrow money to finance their spending programs.
Economic growth measures real GDP per capita growth rate over time.
Policies affecting productivity and labor force participation drive real GDP per capita growth.
Current account tracks exports, income flows, and unilateral transfers between nations.
Exchange rate is the price of one currency expressed in another currency.
Currency demand derives from goods, services, assets; inverse exchange rate relationship.
Currency demand and supply shifts from trade and assets alter equilibrium exchange rates.
Currency appreciation decreases exports and increases imports, reducing net exports.
Higher real interest rates attract financial capital flows between countries.
Bonds and stocks serve as alternative financial assets to liquid money.
Currency depreciation increases exports and decreases imports, raising net exports.
Long-run equilibrium occurs where AD and SRAS intersect the LRAS curve.
Labor force participation rate shows percentage of adults in labor force.
Nominal rate equals expected real rate plus expected inflation expectations.
Consumer income and other factors shift the entire market demand curve.
Three effects explain why AD curve slopes downward: wealth, interest rate, exchange rate.
Money functions as exchange medium, accounting unit, and value storage.
Currency supply stems from payments abroad; positive exchange rate relationship.
Infrastructure and technology investments by government promote economic growth.
Aggregate supply shocks move output and prices in opposite directions.
Banks operate under fractional reserve banking system.
PPC illustrates scarcity, opportunity cost, efficiency, resource utilization, growth.
Business cycles consist of two phases: recession and expansion.
Loanable funds model demonstrates government borrowing effects on interest rates and investment.
Money supply changes don't affect real output at full employment long-term.
Circular flow diagrams represent GDP as income and expenditure flows.
LRAS shifts reflect changes in potential output and economic growth rates.
Fiscal policy affects aggregate demand, output, prices, and currency exchange rates.
Money supply is independent of nominal interest rate.
Government spending and taxes/transfers are fiscal policy tools.
Employment and output correlate directly; firms need more workers for increased production.
Price adjustments restore equilibrium when markets experience surpluses or shortages.
Central banks control short-run interest rates, influencing net capital flows.
Monetary policy tools include discount rates, interest rates, open market operations, reserve ratios.
Currency appreciation means increased value; depreciation means decreased value relative to other currencies.
Sticky wages and prices explain upward slope of SRAS curve.
CPI compares fixed basket costs across years relative to base year.
Changes in production costs and other factors shift the entire supply curve.
Budget deficits accumulate as additions to the national debt.
Comparative advantage: producing goods at lower opportunity cost than competitors.
Current account can run surpluses or deficits; balance of trade is a CA component.
Falling tax revenues during recessions sustain consumption and economic activity.
LRAS curve represents maximum sustainable capacity, parallel to PPC concept.
Expenditure multiplier measures aggregate demand changes from component spending changes.
Higher rates decrease quantity of loanable funds demanded.
Real rate equals nominal rate minus actual inflation rate after the fact.
Specialization by comparative advantage enables trade expanding consumption beyond PPC.
Higher rates increase quantity of loanable funds supplied.
Production cost changes shift SRAS curve, including inflationary expectation changes.
Long-run inflation rate equals money supply growth rate per quantity theory.
PPC shape reflects constant, increasing, or decreasing opportunity costs.
Non-price-level changes in AD components cause the entire curve to shift.
Inflation rate equals percentage change in price indices like CPI.
LRAS is vertical at full employment due to flexible long-run prices and wages.
Short-run output can create inflationary or recessionary output gaps versus full employment.
Inflation results from demand-pull or cost-push mechanisms.
Supply-side fiscal policies influence incentives affecting aggregate demand, supply, and potential output.
Government spending directly affects AD; taxes/transfers affect AD indirectly.
Output per worker measures average labor productivity.
Measured unemployment understates joblessness by excluding discouraged workers.
Rising tax revenues during expansions restrain consumption and prevent overheating.
Capital and financial account records asset transfers and financial capital movements internationally.
Bond prices and interest rates move in opposite directions inversely.
Tax multiplier measures aggregate demand changes resulting from tax changes.
Money supply measurement uses M1 and M2 monetary aggregate categories.
Peak and trough are the turning points marking business cycle transitions.
Crowding out decreases private sector spending when government borrowing increases.
Bank reserves consist of required and excess reserves.
GDP can be measured three ways: expenditures, income, or value-added approaches.
Equilibrium occurs when quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.
Supply and demand shifts create new market equilibrium prices and quantities.
Money market equilibrium occurs when quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.
Monetary policy influences demand, output, prices, interest rates, and exchange rates.
Open market purchases increase reserves and monetary base; sales decrease them.
Interest payments on debt grow, crowding out alternative government spending.
Output gap measures the difference between actual and potential output.
Productivity depends on technology and physical and human capital per worker.
Both multipliers depend on the marginal propensity to consume value.
Short-run inflation-unemployment trade-off exists along SRAS curve movement.
Government spending multiplier exceeds the tax multiplier.
Transfer payments from social programs automatically stabilize income and spending.
Holding money costs the forgone interest from alternative financial assets.
Capital and financial account shows surpluses for inflows and deficits for outflows.
Monetary base comprises currency in circulation plus bank reserves.
Excess reserves enable banks to expand the money supply.
Disequilibrium causes surpluses or shortages; market forces restore equilibrium.
Money multiplier amplifies monetary base changes on money supply in limited-reserve economies.
National savings equals public savings plus private savings in closed economy.
PPC shifts with factor changes and technology/productivity improvements.
Terms of trade based on comparative advantage enable mutually beneficial exchanges.
Three main unemployment types: cyclical, frictional, and structural.
Long-term crowding out reduces capital accumulation and slows economic growth.
Real variables equal nominal variables adjusted for price level changes.
Market forces correct surpluses and shortages, restoring equilibrium exchange rates.
CPI has shortcomings like substitution bias, overstating true inflation.
Money multiplier measures ratio of money supply to base.
Balance of payments system records all international transactions combining current and CFA accounts.
Output per capita increases with physical and human capital per capita.
Economic growth causes outward PPC shift.
MPC equals spending change divided by income change; MPC plus MPS equals one.
Open economy investment includes national savings and net capital inflow.
Central banks target overnight interbank lending rates as their policy rate.
Shifts in money demand and supply change equilibrium nominal interest rate.
Potential output equals full-employment output at natural unemployment rate.
Fiscal policy restores full employment during recessionary or inflationary gaps.
Natural unemployment rate equals frictional plus structural unemployment.
Equilibrium occurs when quantity demanded equals quantity supplied of funds.
Central banks influence nominal rates via money supply or administered interest rates.
BOP credits represent inflows, debits represent outflows; combined accounts must balance to zero.
Money multiplier determines magnitude of money supply expansion.
Cyclical unemployment is deviation from natural rate.
Fiscal policy affects aggregate demand, real output, and price level.
Outward PPC shifts correspond to rightward LRAS curve shifts.
Market forces adjust rates to eliminate surpluses and shortages.
Natural unemployment rate changes over time with labor force characteristics.
Monetary policy addresses output gaps to restore full employment.
AD-AS model demonstrates short-run fiscal policy effects.
Maximum money multiplier equals one divided by reserve ratio.
Loanable funds market shows fiscal policy effects on interest rates.
Monetary policy affects interest rates, aggregate demand, output, and prices.
Discretionary fiscal policy experiences lags from decision and implementation delays.
Simple multiplier may overstate expansion due to bank and public behavior.
Multiple models demonstrate short-run effects of monetary policy actions.
Demand and supply shifts change equilibrium rate and quantity of funds.
Monetary policy lags occur from recognition and adjustment delays.
